results.possibleIdentity: Henry House is the fictional protagonist and artist of the historical novel "The Irresistible Henry House" (2010) by Lisa Grunwald. In the novel, Henry is an artist and animator who is active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. His career includes working as an animator for Disney in California (notably on the 1964 film Mary Poppins) and later moving to London in the late 1960s to work on the animation for the Beatles' psychedelic film "Yellow Submarine." The novel tracks his life from 1946 through the 1970s in New York City, California, and London. While the character is fictional, the novel places him within the real-world historical art and animation scenes of the era, and his association with "Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.)" may be a confusion with E.A.T.'s real-life headquarters at "Automation House" in New York City or the character's presence in the experimental circles of that time.

The name "Henry House" may refer to a confusion between an artist and the historically significant residence of Henry Geldzahler (1935–1994), an influential curator and central figure in the New York art world during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Geldzahler’s home, often referred to as "Henry's house," was a gathering place for experimental artists associated with the 1960s underground scene, including Andy Warhol, Brigid Berlin, and David Hockney. Hockney famously produced a work titled "Chair in Henry's House" (1976). Geldzahler himself was a key supporter of experimental movements and collaborated with numerous artists and organizations, including those active within the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) orbit, though he was primarily known as a curator and commissioner rather than a practicing artist himself.

results.possibleIdentity: Mark Sharp was a prominent architect based in Vancouver, British Columbia, active during the mid-20th century. He was the son of G.L. Thornton Sharp, a founding partner of the influential Canadian architectural firm Sharp & Thompson (later renamed Thompson, Berwick, Pratt & Partners). Mark Sharp became a partner in the firm himself, working alongside notable figures like Fred Thornton Hollingsworth and Ned Pratt. The firm was central to the West Coast Modern style and worked on technologically advanced projects such as the BC Electric Building (1957), which utilized modern curtain-wall construction. While primarily known as an architect, he was part of a multidisciplinary professional circle in Vancouver that frequently collaborated across the arts, music, and design, a spirit aligned with that of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Mark Sharp is a mixed-media artist based in North America whose work has been featured in New York galleries such as the Walter Wickiser Gallery. His artistic practice involves creating 'painted, stained, and sewn collage-like paintings' that have been compared by critics to the styles of Helen Frankenthaler, Lucas Samaras, and Robert Rauschenberg. His work often explores nature and landscapes through what has been described as a 'prism of personal, political or technological concerns.' His exhibitions, such as 'Non Objectivity IV' (2016) and 'Altered States' (2015), suggest a long-standing interest in the intersection of material experimentation and visual perception, though he is more clearly documented in contemporary records than in historical E.A.T. rosters from the late 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Rosemary Beresford is a New Zealand-based arts and theatre practitioner, academic, and author who has been active since at least the late 1960s. After living in London in the 1960s, she moved to Dunedin, New Zealand, in 1970 with her husband, Brian Beresford (a musician and theatre director). She became a prominent figure in the Dunedin arts community, particularly known as a "stalwart" and long-term committee member of the Globe Theatre. Her artistic involvement includes theatre production, cultural event organization, and supporting multi-media installations, such as serving as a contact for Lyn Plummer's multi-media installation "Cantata: a play of the trace II" at the Hocken Library. Professionally, she was an academic at the University of Otago's School of Pharmacy (joining in 1982), where she specialized in the history of pharmacy and the application of technology, specifically artificial neural networks (ANN) and biologically inspired computer programs. Her technical expertise and involvement in the avant-garde theatre scene align with the interdisciplinary goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which promoted collaborations between the arts and sciences during the late 1960s and 1970s. She was appointed an Officer of the New Zealand Order of Merit (ONZM) in 2007 for her services to medicine.

results.possibleIdentity: Hermann Amann (1934–2020) was a German-French painter and sculptor who was a central figure in the Paris art scene from 1960 onwards. He is highly likely the artist in question given his active period in the late 1960s and 1970s and his leadership in artistic groups that bridged the gap between traditional media and new technologies. In 1971, Amann founded the movement "Jacob ou la Persuasion," which featured collaborations with prominent pioneers of computer art, most notably Manfred Mohr and Jean-Claude Marquette. This association with early digital art pioneers and the technological exploration of artistic mediums (such as his work with "Nouvelle Pigmentation" and polymer gels) aligns with the ethos of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His work transitioned from figurative expressionism in the early 1960s to a focus on abstract constructivism and color theory, often utilizing technical advancements in pigments. Amann organized numerous exhibitions and conferences throughout the 1970s that explored the intersection of color, form, and material science.

Hans Amann (active 1960s–1970s) was a German engineer and professor of Marine Technology who conducted significant "experiments" in ocean engineering and deep-sea technology during the 1970s. While primarily identified as a technical scientist and professor at the Technical University of Berlin, he is a plausible candidate for the "H. Amann" associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as the organization was specifically designed to facilitate collaborations between artists and engineers. His documented participation in high-profile technical experiments and projects involving complex machines and international technological planning in the 1970s reflects the professional background of many E.A.T. technical collaborators.
